verses.text: Abijam reigns in Judah and walks in his father's sins, his heart not perfect with God; yet for David's sake the LORD gives him 'a lamp in Jerusalem,' keeping the line. The promise to David holds up an unworthy heir. God's faithfulness, not man's merit, preserves the covenant line.

Abijam reigns in Judah and walks in his father's sins, his heart not perfect with God; yet for David's sake the LORD gives him 'a lamp in Jerusalem,' keeping the line. The promise to David holds up an unworthy heir. God's faithfulness, not man's merit, preserves the covenant line.

Abijam reigns in Judah and walks in his father's sins, his heart not perfect with God; yet for David's sake the LORD gives him 'a lamp in Jerusalem,' keeping the line. The promise to David holds up an unworthy heir. God's faithfulness, not man's merit, preserves the covenant line.

There is war between Abijam and Jeroboam, and Abijam dies, succeeded by Asa. The brief, flawed reign passes. Kings rise and fall in quick succession, but God's purpose moves steadily on beneath the turning of the years.

There is war between Abijam and Jeroboam, and Abijam dies, succeeded by Asa. The brief, flawed reign passes. Kings rise and fall in quick succession, but God's purpose moves steadily on beneath the turning of the years.

There is war between Abijam and Jeroboam, and Abijam dies, succeeded by Asa. The brief, flawed reign passes. Kings rise and fall in quick succession, but God's purpose moves steadily on beneath the turning of the years.

Asa does right, removing the idols his fathers made, even deposing his own grandmother for her idol and burning it. He cleanses the land with courage. True reform spares no one, not even family; love for God outweighs every tie that would shield sin.

Asa does right, removing the idols his fathers made, even deposing his own grandmother for her idol and burning it. He cleanses the land with courage. True reform spares no one, not even family; love for God outweighs every tie that would shield sin.

Though the high places are not all removed, 'Asa's heart was perfect with the LORD all his days,' and he brings dedicated treasures into God's house. His reform is imperfect yet sincere. God accepts a heart wholly His, even where the work of reform is left unfinished.

Though the high places are not all removed, 'Asa's heart was perfect with the LORD all his days,' and he brings dedicated treasures into God's house. His reform is imperfect yet sincere. God accepts a heart wholly His, even where the work of reform is left unfinished.

Pressed by Baasha of Israel, Asa strips the temple treasures to hire Syria's help against him. The good king, in fear, leans on a pagan alliance rather than on God. Even the faithful can stumble into trusting human schemes when they forget to trust the LORD.

Pressed by Baasha of Israel, Asa strips the temple treasures to hire Syria's help against him. The good king, in fear, leans on a pagan alliance rather than on God. Even the faithful can stumble into trusting human schemes when they forget to trust the LORD.

The Syrian alliance works, Baasha withdraws, and Asa fortifies his border. The plan succeeds outwardly — yet it was a step away from faith. Worldly help may bring short-term relief while quietly weakening the soul's reliance on God.

Asa, great in his deeds, is diseased in his feet in old age, then dies and is succeeded by his son Jehoshaphat. A long, mostly faithful reign ends in weakness. Even good men carry their frailties to the grave; their hope, like ours, rests not in themselves but in God.

Asa, great in his deeds, is diseased in his feet in old age, then dies and is succeeded by his son Jehoshaphat. A long, mostly faithful reign ends in weakness. Even good men carry their frailties to the grave; their hope, like ours, rests not in themselves but in God.

In Israel, Nadab reigns two years and does evil, walking in his father Jeroboam's sin. The rot continues unbroken in the north. A dynasty built on false worship breeds successors who know no other way.

In Israel, Nadab reigns two years and does evil, walking in his father Jeroboam's sin. The rot continues unbroken in the north. A dynasty built on false worship breeds successors who know no other way.

Baasha conspires, kills Nadab, and destroys the whole house of Jeroboam, leaving none alive — fulfilling exactly what Ahijah foretold. God's word of judgment comes to pass through human ambition. Even a usurper's bloody hand can perform the LORD's sentence.

Baasha conspires, kills Nadab, and destroys the whole house of Jeroboam, leaving none alive — fulfilling exactly what Ahijah foretold. God's word of judgment comes to pass through human ambition. Even a usurper's bloody hand can perform the LORD's sentence.

But Baasha himself does evil and walks in Jeroboam's sin, so the kingdom he seized by judgment is already under judgment. He destroyed one wicked house only to build another. Those who carry out God's judgment yet refuse to learn from it fall under it in turn.
